title: tensor_pdist severs autograd; composable through index_select for finite p > 0
description:
tensor_pdist at lib.rs:4349 computes pairwise L_p distances between rows of a 2-D input. For finite p > 0 the math is composable through autograd-aware primitives (index_select pairs of rows, abs, pow, sum_dim, pow). For p = inf it's argmax-routing; for p = 0 it's a non-differentiable counting metric.

Strategy: build row_i and row_j index tensors of length N*(N-1)/2 each, use tensor_index_select to gather row pairs, then compute:
   diff = left - right
   per_pair = (sum_k |diff[k]|^p)^(1/p)
All ops autograd-aware. Add fail-loud guards for p == 0 (counting metric, non-diff) and p == +inf (could be added with max_dim but defer).
notes: Fixed in this commit. For finite p > 0, composed tensor_pdist through index_select + sub + abs + pow + sum_dim + pow primitives. Pre-built row_i / row_j index tensors of length N*(N-1)/2 enumerate the upper-triangular pairs; index_select gathers the row-pair tensors so subsequent ops broadcast naturally. p == 0 (counting metric) and p == +inf (max-routing) now fail-loud on requires_grad=true rather than silently severing — autograd implementations for those cases are deferred. Added pdist_l2_propagates_gradient_through_input regression test. ft-api lib tests: 1169.

title: tensor_kthvalue severs autograd; index-routing backward needed
description:
tensor_kthvalue at lib.rs:12512 returns the k-th smallest element of a 1-D tensor. The value branch is just input[idx] where idx is the rank-k sorted index. PyTorch's torch.kthvalue IS differentiable: grad_input[idx] = grad_value, all other positions zero.

Wrap value branch in tensor_apply_function with this single-index routing backward. Index branch stays a non-grad leaf (integer).
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped tensor_kthvalue's value branch in tensor_apply_function with single-index routing backward: grad_input[idx] = grad_value where idx is the sort-rank-k index. The index branch stays a non-grad leaf. Added kthvalue_propagates_gradient_to_routed_index regression test (x=[3,1,4,1,5], k=3 → 3rd smallest is at idx 0 with value 3, grad_input = [1,0,0,0,0]). ft-api lib tests: 1163.

title: Investigate persistent beads SQLite integrity warning after clean rebuild
description:
## Context
After a full non-destructive rebuild of `.beads/beads.db` from canonical `issues.jsonl`, `br doctor --json` still reports:

`sqlite.integrity_check: database disk image is malformed: page 17 is never used`

This persists even on the freshly rebuilt DB, while all operational checks are otherwise healthy (`br ready --json` is empty, sync metadata matches, blocked cache is not stale).

## Goal
Determine whether this warning reflects:
1. a genuine low-level SQLite file/layout problem,
2. expected frankensqlite behavior that `br doctor` should not flag as suspicious, or
3. a beads/br bug in the integrity-check path.

## Acceptance
- Reproduce the warning on the current canonical DB
- Compare against a freshly rebuilt temp DB
- Gather enough evidence to classify the issue as workspace-local vs tool-level
- If it is tool-level or not safely fixable from this repo, document the finding clearly and close or hand off with evidence
close_reason: Reproduced the integrity warning on canonical and rebuilt DBs, proved a plain SQLite VACUUM clears it, then observed br doctor --repair reintroduce the warning on a different page number while keeping logical metadata healthy; this classifies the issue as a tool-level beads/frankensqlite write-path problem rather than repo-local issue-data corruption

description: These ft-api ops all extract tensor values via self.tensor_values + return self.tensor_tape.leaf(... false), silently severing the autograd tape on tracked inputs (the same pattern recently fixed for SELU / CELU / Threshold / Hardtanh / Unfold / Fold / index_* / scatter_* / masked_scatter / PReLU + 6 shrink-family activations). Some have explicit fail-loud guards (logit doesn't, erfinv does, digamma doesn't, etc.) but the contract is inconsistent. Pick each off and either compose through autograd primitives (preferred where the math admits it) or add a uniform fail-loud guard. PyTorch's torch.cov / torch.corrcoef / torch.special.{logit,digamma,polygamma,...} are all differentiable. Children: each op gets its own fix bead.
notes: All children resolved across multiple commits: cov/corrcoef/mode/quantile/logit/xlogy/xlog1py/entr composed through autograd primitives in earlier rounds; gammaln (7cc0b45), digamma (86fb29d), polygamma + multigammaln (bd2817b), erfinv (ac049c6), logcumsumexp (ec75d35) wrapped in tensor_apply_function with analytical backwards. ft-api lib tests now at 1135 passing.

title: Add custom autograd Function API (torch.autograd.Function equivalent)
description:
Users cannot currently define custom backward functions. This is arguably the single most impactful missing feature for real-world use — without it, any layer whose backward isn't built into FrankenTorch's op set is impossible to implement.

PyTorch's torch.autograd.Function API allows users to:
1. Define a custom forward() that registers itself on the autograd tape
2. Define a custom backward() that computes gradients for the custom op
3. Save tensors for backward via ctx.save_for_backward()

Implementation plan:
1. Add a CustomFunction trait in ft-autograd with forward/backward methods
2. Add a TensorNodeOp::Custom variant to the tensor tape
3. Add session.apply_custom_function() to ft-api that invokes the user's forward, registers the op on the tape, and stores the backward closure
4. During backward traversal, call the user's backward function when encountering Custom nodes
5. Handle save_for_backward by capturing TensorNodeIds in the closure context

Key design decisions:
- The backward closure must receive upstream gradient and return downstream gradients
- Must respect no_grad context (custom forward inside no_grad should not record on tape)
- Must work with the existing deterministic backward scheduler
- Error handling: user backward functions should return Result, not panic

Tests:
- Unit test: custom ReLU equivalent (forward: max(0,x), backward: 1 if x>0 else 0)
- Unit test: custom function with multiple inputs/outputs
- Unit test: custom function inside no_grad context (should not record)
- Unit test: custom function backward produces correct gradients (numerical gradient check)
- E2E test: train a model with a custom layer and verify loss decreases
- Edge case: nested custom functions
- Edge case: custom function that saves tensors for backward
close_reason: Core functionality already exists: tensor_apply_function() with FunctionCtx (save_for_backward, needs_input_grad) + backward closure. Extensive tests in ft-autograd (lines 17484+) and used internally by ft-api for ~12 ops. The closure-based API is the Rust-idiomatic equivalent of PyTorch's class-based torch.autograd.Function. A trait wrapper could be added later for API sugar but is not blocking.

title: Severed-autograd loss umbrella: focal, poisson_nll, gaussian_nll
description:
Three more loss functions in ft-api that extract values, compute the per-element loss in plain f64, build a requires_grad=false leaf, then take tensor_mean — same severed-autograd pattern recently fixed for cross_entropy_with_smoothing / multilabel_soft_margin / soft_margin / multi_margin. The tensor_mean step preserves autograd from that point but the prior leaf has already severed the tape — so input never receives gradient.

Affected:
  - focal_loss (lib.rs:10324) — used in dense object detection (RetinaNet etc.)
  - poisson_nll_loss (lib.rs:10357) — count regression
  - gaussian_nll_loss (lib.rs:10398) — variational / probabilistic regression

Each has a closed-form composition through autograd-aware primitives; Stirling / 2pi correction terms are constants and can be added as scalars without affecting the tape.

Children: each loss gets its own fix bead. Surfaced during continuing /reality-check pass.
notes: All children closed: poisson_nll (zl3n, 8d33f51), gaussian_nll (vjow, this commit), focal_loss (m6uk, this commit).

title: Activation functions lack subprocess conformance: sigmoid, tanh, silu, mish
description:
Existing subprocess conformance suite covers gelu, softplus, exp/log/expm1/log1p/erf/erfc/lgamma/trig/hyperbolic, but the workhorse activations sigmoid / tanh / silu / mish have no scipy or libm reference comparison. PyTorch parity for these matters because:
- sigmoid (= 1/(1+exp(-x)) = expit) is the building block for binary classification, gating, attention
- tanh is the workhorse for RNN/LSTM
- silu/swish (= x*sigmoid(x)) is used in transformer FFN
- mish (= x*tanh(softplus(x))) is used in detection / vision

Reference: scipy.special.expit (sigmoid), numpy.tanh, numpy compositions for silu and mish. ULP-tolerant comparison; FrankenTorch composes these through libm exp/tanh which should match within ~1 ULP on smooth interior plus edge cases for ±inf / NaN / large-magnitude saturation.

Add torch_activations_libm_subprocess_conformance covering all four with ~30 inputs spanning negative / zero / positive / large / Inf / NaN. Filed during testing-conformance pass.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Added torch_activations_scipy_subprocess_conformance covering sigmoid (vs scipy.special.expit), tanh (vs numpy.tanh), silu = x*sigmoid(x), and mish = x*tanh(softplus(x)) across 30 inputs spanning negative / zero / positive / saturation (±50, ±100, ±700) / Inf / NaN. ULP-tolerant comparator: 4 ULP for sigmoid/tanh, 16 ULP for the composed silu/mish. Subprocess conformance count now 33 (was 32).

title: tensor_interpolate linear/bilinear/trilinear autograd via index_select + weighted sum
description:
After frankentorch-ov0v added a fail-loud guard, tensor_interpolate with mode='linear'/'bilinear'/'trilinear' is unusable in autograd flows. Each output position is a weighted sum of 2 (linear), 4 (bilinear), or 8 (trilinear) input pixels — compose via tensor_reshape + tensor_index_select (per corner) + tensor_mul (with non-grad per-position weight tensor) + tensor_add.

Bicubic uses 16 corners with cubic Hermite spline weights — defer.

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_interpolate (line 14674) — interpolate_linear_1d, interpolate_bilinear, interpolate_trilinear paths.

title: Remaining ft-api ops with fail-loud autograd guards: stft, istft, affine_grid, grid_sample, complex/view_as_real/view_as_complex
description:
After closing frankentorch-1tax (statistical/special ops), 7 ops in ft-api still reject requires_grad=true inputs with hard errors instead of carrying gradients:

- stft (line 1248) — short-time Fourier transform; PyTorch's torch.stft supports backward via inverse FFT.
- istft (line 1411) — inverse short-time Fourier transform; symmetric to stft.
- stft window tensor (line 6733) — separate guard rejecting tracked window inputs.
- affine_grid (line 7774) — torch.nn.functional.affine_grid supports backward.
- grid_sample (line 7905) — torch.nn.functional.grid_sample supports backward.
- complex (line 994/1001) — torch.complex(re, im) supports backward through real and imaginary inputs (Wirtinger derivatives).
- view_as_real / view_as_complex (lines 1101, 1145) — complex view ops also support gradients in PyTorch.

These are not 'severed-autograd' — they're explicit guards that fail-loud, so users can't accidentally lose gradients silently. But they DO block training paths that would work in PyTorch (e.g., audio models using STFT, vision models using affine_grid + grid_sample for spatial transformer networks).

Each child needs its own fix bead. Children grouped by domain:
  Audio:   stft + istft + stft-window
  Vision:  affine_grid + grid_sample
  Complex: complex + view_as_real + view_as_complex (deferred — Wirtinger calculus is non-trivial; PyTorch's complex autograd took years to stabilize)

Priority for audio/vision (P2): real downstream training paths blocked. Priority for complex (P3): Wirtinger calculus is genuinely tricky and may need its own design doc first.
notes:
affine_grid slice closed in 669df96 (frankentorch-bfj5). Remaining children that still need work:
  - stft / istft / stft window — Audio. Backward via inverse FFT or windowed-overlap-add gradient. Non-trivial.
  - grid_sample (bilinear mode) — Vision. Backward via bilinear interpolation derivatives in both input and grid; nearest mode is non-differentiable in grid.
  - complex / view_as_real / view_as_complex — Wirtinger calculus. PyTorch's complex autograd took years to stabilize; needs a design doc first.

Each remaining child should get its own bead when picked up. Priorities:
  - stft + istft + stft-window: P2 (audio training paths blocked)
  - grid_sample: P2 (vision training paths blocked, e.g. spatial transformer networks)
  - complex/Wirtinger: P3 (defer until design doc)

title: Add basic quantization support (torch.quantization equivalent)
description:
PyTorch's quantization allows running models with INT8 weights for faster inference and smaller model size. This is increasingly important for edge deployment.

Implementation plan (CPU quantization only):
1. Add quantized dtypes to ft-core: QInt8, QUInt8 (with scale and zero_point metadata)
2. Add QuantizedTensorMeta extending TensorMeta with quantization parameters
3. Add quantize/dequantize functions:
   - session.quantize_per_tensor(input, scale, zero_point, dtype) -> quantized tensor
   - session.dequantize(quantized) -> float tensor
4. Add quantized linear layer (QuantizedLinear) in ft-nn:
   - Stores INT8 weights + F32 bias
   - Forward: dequantize weights -> matmul -> add bias (or fused quantized matmul if we add a kernel)
5. Add basic observer (MinMaxObserver) for calibration:
   - Tracks running min/max of tensor values
   - Computes optimal scale/zero_point
6. Add post-training quantization workflow:
   - Calibrate observers on representative data
   - Convert float model to quantized model

Tests:
- Unit test: quantize then dequantize recovers original values (within quantization error)
- Unit test: quantized linear forward matches dequantize-then-matmul
- Unit test: MinMaxObserver correctly tracks min/max
- Unit test: scale/zero_point calculation is correct
- E2E test: train a float model, quantize it, verify inference output is close to float model
- Edge case: quantize with scale=0 should error, not divide by zero
close_reason: Implemented MVP fake-quantization slice: tensor_quantize_per_tensor / tensor_dequantize_per_tensor / tensor_fake_quantize_per_tensor on FrankenTorchSession (composes round + clamp + scalar shift; STE backward via tensor_round); MinMaxObserver and QParams with qint8/quint8 helpers in ft-nn; calibrated observer -> qparams -> fake_quantize E2E test. Deferred scope (QInt8/QUInt8 storage dtypes, QuantizedLinear, per-channel quantization) tracked in follow-up bead.

title: multilabel_soft_margin_loss severs autograd; delegate to bce_with_logits_loss
description:
multilabel_soft_margin_loss at lib.rs:10507 implements the per-element BCE-with-logits formula manually (max(x,0) - x*t + log1p(exp(-|x|))), accumulates in plain f64, and rebuilds a requires_grad=false leaf — same severed-autograd pattern fixed across the recent ft-api cleanup.

For a 2-D input [batch, classes] the multilabel_soft_margin_loss formula reduces to mean over all (i, j) of BCE_with_logits(x[i,j], t[i,j]), which is exactly what bce_with_logits_loss computes (the latter is autograd-aware via composition through tensor_relu + tensor_abs + tensor_log1p + tensor_mean).

Fix: delegate to self.bce_with_logits_loss(input, target) with the existing 2-D shape validation. Same arithmetic, same numerical stability via log1p(exp(-|x|)), but with the autograd tape preserved.

Sister of frankentorch-ilwr (cross_entropy with smoothing severance). Surfaced during /reality-check pass.
notes:
Fixed in this commit. Replaced the manual per-element BCE accumulator with delegation to bce_with_logits_loss, which already implements the same numerically-stable formula max(x, 0) - x*t + log1p(exp(-|x|)) via composition through tensor_relu + tensor_abs + tensor_log1p + tensor_mean (all autograd-aware). For shape [batch, classes] both losses produce identical scalars (mean over all elements) and now both share gradient flow.

Added multilabel_soft_margin_loss_propagates_gradient_via_bce_with_logits regression test. Tests at x = ±1 to avoid the relu/abs corner at x=0 where FrankenTorch's sub-gradient convention differs slightly from the smooth sigmoid(x)-t form. ft-api lib tests: 1152.

title: tensor_linalg_vector_norm severs autograd via tensor_values + non-grad rebuild
description:
tensor_linalg_vector_norm in ft-api (line 12741) extracts tensor_values, computes the chosen norm in plain f64, and rebuilds via session.tensor_variable(vec![norm], vec![1], false). This silently severs autograd through every vector_norm computation — used widely in normalization, loss functions, gradient clipping, etc.

Fix: compose through autograd-aware primitives. For each ord:
- ord = inf: tensor_abs + tensor_max (max is differentiable at the strict argmax)
- ord = -inf: tensor_abs + tensor_min
- ord = 0: non-differentiable (integer count) — keep severed but add fail-loud guard for requires_grad
- ord = p (positive finite): tensor_abs + tensor_pow(p) + tensor_sum + tensor_pow(1/p)

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_linalg_vector_norm (line 12741).

title: tensor_repeat_interleave severs autograd; sum-of-R-grad backward
description:
tensor_repeat_interleave at lib.rs:11097 takes a 1-D input and repeats each element R times consecutively. PyTorch's torch.repeat_interleave IS differentiable.

Backward: grad_input[i] = sum over j where j // R == i of grad_output[j]. Each input element gets the sum of the R consecutive output gradients that came from it.

Wrap in tensor_apply_function.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped tensor_repeat_interleave in tensor_apply_function with sum-of-R backward: grad_input[i] = sum_{j: j / R == i} grad_output[j]. Each input element gets the sum of the R consecutive output gradients that came from it. Preserved the byte-size overflow check (output count alone can fit in usize even when out_len * 8 bytes overflows). Added repeat_interleave_propagates_gradient_via_sum_of_repeats regression test (x=[3,5], R=4 → grad=[4,4]). ft-api lib tests: 1165.

title: RaptorQ sidecar generation accepts unbounded repair symbol counts
description: Reviewer finding in crates/ft-serialize/src/lib.rs and crates/ft-conformance/src/bin/run_raptorq_durability_pipeline.rs. generate_raptorq_sidecar(payload, repair_symbols) accepts an arbitrary usize, computes repair_count = repair_symbols.max(...).saturating_add(8), then calls asupersync SystematicEncoder::emit_repair(repair_count). asupersync 0.3.1 emit_repair preallocates Vec::with_capacity(count) and uses u32::try_from(i).expect("repair count exceeds u32") / checked_add(...).expect(...) inside the loop. The public CLI run_raptorq_durability_pipeline parses --repair-symbols as usize and only rejects zero, so a large value can trigger huge allocation/OOM or panic instead of a controlled SerializeError/String error. Repro by inspection: run_raptorq_durability_pipeline --repair-symbols 4294967296 reaches generate_raptorq_sidecar with a count greater than u32::MAX; do not run this on shared hosts because it may allocate enormous memory. Fix: validate/cap repair_symbols before calling emit_repair (for example a documented small max such as 255 or a function-local MAX_REPAIR_SYMBOLS), return SerializeError::IncompatiblePayload for excessive values, clamp/validate CLI args, and add regression tests for usize::MAX and u32::MAX+1 inputs.
close_reason: Added MAX_RAPTORQ_REPAIR_SYMBOLS = 1024 (user-input cap) and an internal RAPTORQ_REPAIR_COUNT_HARD_CAP = 65536 to bound the computed repair_count after k_prime padding + headroom. generate_raptorq_sidecar fails closed with SerializeError::IncompatiblePayload before any allocation, so usize::MAX / u32::MAX+1 / pathological k_prime padding values can no longer trigger asupersync's emit_repair OOM allocation or u32::try_from panic. CLI run_raptorq_durability_pipeline mirrors the input cap for a user-facing diagnostic. Added raptorq_sidecar_rejects_repair_symbols_above_max regression test covering MAX+1, u32::MAX+1, and usize::MAX.

title: AlphaDropout in ft-nn severs autograd; SELU networks broken
description:
AlphaDropout at ft-nn/src/lib.rs:2146 implements its forward by extracting input values, applying the alpha-dropout transform in plain f64, and rebuilding a requires_grad=false leaf. PyTorch's torch.nn.AlphaDropout IS differentiable. Severs autograd through every AlphaDropout layer.

AlphaDropout is paired with SELU activations to maintain mean/variance through dropout — used in self-normalizing networks (Klambauer et al. 2017). Models using SELU + AlphaDropout couldn't actually train.

Math: y = a * (mask * x + (1 - mask) * sat) + a * p * sat
where mask is the random keep-or-drop mask, sat is the saturation value, a is a precomputed scale.

Composable through autograd primitives: mask leaf (non-grad) + tensor_mul (autograd) for the input branch, constant offsets via full + tensor_add for the rest.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed AlphaDropout forward through autograd-aware primitives: random mask leaf (non-grad) + tensor_mul against input + tensor_sub for inverse mask + scaled drop branch + affine offset. The transform y = a * (mask * x + (1-mask) * sat) + a*p*sat is preserved exactly but the autograd tape now flows through input. ft-nn lib tests: 517.

title: tensor_scatter_reduce amax/amin autograd via argmax-routing masks + scatter_add
description:
After uzca added mean autograd, only prod/amax/amin still fail loud. amax and amin can be implemented via argmax-routing:

1. Compute argmax_per_position in plain f64 (non-diff)
2. mask_input[i] = 1 if input[i] is the max (won), else 0
3. mask_src[j] = 1 if src[j] is the max at position index[j], else 0
4. result = scatter_add(mul(mask_input, input), dim, index, mul(mask_src, src))

For each output position, exactly one of input[i] or one src[j]→i 'wins'; the other terms are zeroed by the masks. Backward correctly routes gradient to the winning element via mul backward. Same pattern works for amin (find argmin instead).

prod still needs special handling (divide-back is unstable at zero); defer.

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_scatter_reduce (line 16249).

title: pad_sequence severs autograd via tensor_values + non-grad rebuild
description:
pad_sequence in ft-nn (line ~13596) extracts tensor_values from each input sequence, builds a flat output buffer with placeholder padding values, then rebuilds via session.tensor_variable(output, out_shape, false). This silently severs autograd through pad_sequence — gradients can't flow back to any input sequence. Breaks training for any RNN/LSTM/GRU pipeline that uses torch.nn.utils.rnn.pad_sequence on variable-length data.

Fix: each sequence can be padded to max_len via tensor_pad (autograd-aware), then stacked along the new batch dimension. The padding value is configurable; if non-zero, compose: padded = tensor_pad(seq, [...], 0.0) + (1 - mask) * padding_value where mask is the non-grad tensor marking the original positions. For padding_value=0.0 (the common case), tensor_pad alone suffices.

Affects: crates/ft-nn/src/lib.rs pad_sequence (line 13596 region).

title: tensor_cartesian_prod severs autograd; per-tensor scatter backward
description:
tensor_cartesian_prod at lib.rs:4065 takes 1-D tensors and emits all combinations as a 2-D output. Each output cell is a copy of one input element via a deterministic odometer mapping. PyTorch's torch.cartesian_prod IS differentiable.

Backward: for each input tensor k with N_k entries,
   grad_input_k[i] = sum over rows where indices[k] == i of grad_output[row, k]

Each input element appears in (total_rows / N_k) rows, so each contribution is sum of that many grad entries. Wrap in tensor_apply_function with this scatter-per-input backward.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped tensor_cartesian_prod in tensor_apply_function with per-tensor scatter backward. Pre-computed (row, k) → input_index_in_tensor_k mapping is captured by both forward (gather) and backward (scatter) closures so the routing is deterministic and free of per-call recomputation. Backward returns Vec<Option<Vec<f64>>> with one entry per input tensor. Added cartesian_prod_propagates_gradient_to_each_input regression test (a=[1,2], b=[3,4] → grad_a=[2,2], grad_b=[2,2] under sum loss). ft-api lib tests: 1167.

title: functional_dropout severs autograd; mask is supposed to flow gradient through input
description:
functional_dropout at lib.rs:5199 generates random mask values, multiplies in plain f64, and rebuilds a requires_grad=false leaf — silently severing autograd through dropout. Every transformer / convnet training that uses dropout would have gradients of input dropped to zero by the dropout layer's masking. PyTorch's torch.nn.functional.dropout is differentiable: the mask is sampled per-forward (non-differentiable random) but the multiplication input * mask * scale carries gradient through input.

Fix: replace the manual values+leaf assembly with composition through autograd-aware tensor_mul:
   mask_vals = 0 where dropped, scale=1/(1-p) where surviving (random per-element)
   mask = tensor_variable(mask_vals, shape, false)   # non-grad mask is correct
   output = tensor_mul(input, mask)                  # autograd-aware

Same observable forward behavior, but the autograd tape is preserved for the input.

Critical severity — this affects every dropout-using training run. Sister of the recent loss-severance cluster (ilwr / 4liu / tr2a / x9cr). Surfaced during /reality-check pass.
notes:
Fixed in this commit. Replaced functional_dropout's manual mask-and-scale body with composition through tensor_mul against a non-grad random mask. The mask values are 0 (dropped) or scale = 1/(1-p) (kept), generated per-element via the RNG; multiplication preserves the autograd tape so input gradients flow as expected. Same observable forward behavior, but training paths through dropout layers now actually train.

Critical-severity fix: every dropout-using transformer / convnet training was previously getting zero gradient downstream of any dropout layer, making training a no-op for the affected sub-network.

Added functional_dropout_propagates_gradient_through_input regression test (n=100, p=0.5 → grad entries are 0 or 1/(1-p) = 2 element-wise; sanity check that at least 10 survive). ft-api lib tests: 1155.

title: Fix HingeEmbeddingLoss autograd via tensor_where + relu [child of cq0b]
description:
HingeEmbeddingLoss::forward_hinge at lib.rs:11003 computes elementwise:
   loss_i = x_i        if y_i > 0
          = max(0, margin - x_i)  otherwise
Composable: y_pos_mask = (y > 0), then mask * x + (1 - mask) * relu(margin - x). Or tensor_where over the same condition.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed HingeEmbeddingLoss through tensor_relu + mask-based blend (mask is non-grad target-derived; pos_branch = mask*input; neg_branch = (1-mask)*relu(margin-input); loss = mean(pos+neg)). All ops autograd-aware. ft-nn lib tests: 517.

title: tensor_nonzero on 0-d scalar always returns shape [0, 0]
description:
FrankenTorchSession::tensor_nonzero() at crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs:10260 silently returns shape [0, 0] for any 0-d (scalar) input — including non-zero scalars where PyTorch returns shape [1, 0].

Root cause at line 10309:
    let num_nonzero = indices.len().checked_div(ndim).unwrap_or(0);
When ndim == 0 (scalar), the inner stride loop runs 0 times so 'indices' stays empty for any value, and 0.checked_div(0).unwrap_or(0) = 0 — losing the 'is non-zero' signal entirely.

PyTorch parity:
- torch.nonzero(torch.tensor(5.0)) -> shape [1, 0]
- torch.nonzero(torch.tensor(0.0)) -> shape [0, 0]
- torch.nonzero(torch.tensor(float('nan'))) -> shape [1, 0]  (NaN treated as nonzero)

Fix: track non-zero count explicitly instead of inferring it from indices.len() / ndim, and also clean up the related tensor_nonzero_as_tuple which currently returns an empty Vec for any scalar (matches PyTorch '() tuple' but worth documenting).
close_reason: tensor_nonzero now tracks num_nonzero explicitly so 0-d scalars produce the correct PyTorch-equivalent shape: non-zero scalar -> [1, 0], zero scalar -> [0, 0], NaN scalar -> [1, 0] (NaN treated as nonzero). Previously indices.len().checked_div(0).unwrap_or(0) collapsed all scalars to [0, 0]. 3 new scalar tests added; all 1082 ft-api tests pass; clippy + ubs clean.

title: weight_norm_reconstruct severs autograd via tensor_values + non-grad rebuild
description:
weight_norm_reconstruct in ft-nn (line 13718) extracts tensor_values from g and v, computes w = g * v / ||v|| in plain f64, and rebuilds via session.tensor_variable(w_values, v_shape, false). This silently severs autograd: gradients can't flow back to either g (magnitude) or v (direction), breaking training for any model using nn.utils.weight_norm (WaveNet, certain GANs, normalizing-flow variants).

Fix: compose via autograd primitives. Math:
  w[i, ..., dim_idx, ..., j] = g[dim_idx] * v[i, ..., dim_idx, ..., j] / ||v||[dim_idx]
where ||v||[dim_idx] = sqrt(sum over non-dim axes of v^2).

Implementation: permute v so dim is first, reshape to [dim_size, others], compute v*v, sum_dim along axis 1, sqrt, clamp_min(eps); broadcast g and norm to v's permuted shape; divide; multiply; reshape and permute back to v_shape.

Affects: crates/ft-nn/src/lib.rs weight_norm_reconstruct (line 13718)

title: Add metamorphic invariant tests for softmax/log_softmax/transpose/inverse equivalences
description:
Existing fuzz harness in ft-conformance covers 4 ops (abs, add, sum, matmul) with shape/finiteness assertions. There are no metamorphic equivalence tests — checking that two algebraically-equivalent paths produce the same output within ULP tolerance.

Catch-cases this would lock down:
1. exp(log_softmax(x)) ≈ softmax(x)   — composition equivalence
2. log(softmax(x)) ≈ log_softmax(x)   — direct vs explicit
3. softmax(x).sum() ≈ 1.0             — probability invariant
4. softmax(x + c) ≈ softmax(x)        — translation invariance
5. transpose(transpose(x)) ≡ x        — involution
6. matmul(I, A) ≈ A                   — identity invariance
7. matmul(A, A.inv()) ≈ I             — inverse invariance (well-conditioned A)
8. (a + b).sum() ≈ a.sum() + b.sum()  — sum linearity

Each can be a proptest with random valid inputs. The log_softmax precision bug fixed under frankentorch-ebrb would have been caught by (1) and (2) — that's the kind of bug these target.

Filed during /testing-metamorphic round of the multi-pass cycle.
notes:
Fixed in this commit. Added 4 metamorphic property tests inside the existing #[cfg(feature='fuzz')] proptest! block in ft-conformance:
  - fuzz_metamorphic_log_softmax_equals_log_of_softmax (the case that would have caught frankentorch-ebrb at random inputs)
  - fuzz_metamorphic_softmax_sums_to_one (probability-distribution invariant)
  - fuzz_metamorphic_softmax_translation_invariance (softmax(x + c) == softmax(x))
  - fuzz_metamorphic_double_transpose_is_identity (bit-exact involution)
ULP-tolerant comparators with absolute-floor fallbacks. ft-conformance lib tests with --features fuzz: 135 passing.

2. Add TorchScript-like serialization or a compatible alternative representation

title: tensor_bincount severs autograd through weights tensor
description:
ft-api tensor_bincount (line 3651) extracts both input and weight tensor_values, builds the bin sums in plain f64, and rebuilds via tensor_variable(_, _, false). The input (bin indices) is integer-valued and intrinsically non-differentiable, but PyTorch's bincount IS differentiable through the weights — silently severing here breaks training for any use of weighted bincount in custom losses (focal loss in detection, class re-weighting, etc.).

Fix: compose via tensor_scatter_add. Build a zeros tensor of shape [n_bins], use input (cast to f64 index tensor) as scatter index, and weights as src. The unweighted case still returns counts (use ones tensor as src).

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_bincount (line 3651).

title: backward_create_graph silently swallows tensor read errors as empty gradients
description:
TensorTape::backward_create_graph (crates/ft-autograd/src/lib.rs ~12933, ~12958) calls grad_node.tensor.contiguous_values_as_f64().unwrap_or_default() in two places.

If contiguous_values_as_f64() returns an error (e.g. UnsupportedLayout for a non-contiguous result, or StorageSpanOverflow for a malformed meta), unwrap_or_default() yields an empty Vec, and the leaf's gradient silently becomes [].

Risks:
- The TensorBackwardReport reports a None/empty-but-nominally-success gradient.
- persistent_grads accumulator zip-iterates over an empty 'vals', leaving the entry untouched on second-and-later backward passes — silently breaking gradient accumulation across calls.

This violates the project's non-regression rule (autograd correctness outranks kernel speed) by hiding a DenseTensorError as a missing gradient.

Fix: propagate the error via ? into AutogradError (already has From<DenseTensorError>), so a malformed gradient tensor surfaces at the backward boundary instead of being silently dropped.
close_reason: backward_create_graph now propagates DenseTensorError via ? at both gradient-extraction sites instead of unwrap_or_default-ing tensor reads to empty Vec. Malformed grad tensors surface as AutogradError::DenseTensor at the backward boundary, restoring the 'autograd correctness outranks kernel speed' invariant. All 310 ft-autograd + 1082 ft-api tests pass; clippy + ubs clean.

title: Add messages to bare unreachable!() calls (5 sites)
description:
## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) found 26 `unreachable!()` calls across the codebase. 21 already have explanatory messages or surrounding comments. 5 do NOT have messages, making debugging harder if they ever fire due to future code changes.

## Bare unreachable!() sites to fix
These are the sites that lack an explanatory message string:

1. ft-dispatch/src/lib.rs — multiple bare unreachable!() in dispatch routing match arms
2. ft-autograd/src/lib.rs:2947 — bare unreachable!() in backward pass routing

The remaining 21 sites already have messages like `unreachable!("complex dtypes rejected above")` and need no changes.

## Plan
1. For each bare `unreachable!()`, add a message string explaining WHY it is unreachable, e.g.:
   `unreachable!("all dispatch key variants handled above")`
2. Do NOT change logic — only add the message parameter
3. Verify with clippy that no new warnings are introduced

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 26 unreachable!() calls across the workspace have explanatory message strings
- Zero logic changes
- Clippy clean

## Reasoning
If the "impossible" state ever becomes possible due to a new enum variant or refactor, the message tells the developer exactly which invariant was violated. This is a defense-in-depth measure aligned with the DAC correctness doctrine.
close_reason: Added explanatory messages to all remaining bare unreachable!() sites across crates and verified workspace check/clippy via rch

description: Add session-level functional ops matching torch.nn.functional: normalize (Lp normalization along dim), dropout (random zeroing), embedding (lookup table), linear (y=xA^T+b). These are commonly used in custom model forward() methods outside of nn.Module.

title: Add compile-time exhaustiveness guard for DType match arms
description:
## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) found 26 unreachable!() calls. Many exist because match arms on DType handle a subset of variants and use unreachable!() for the rest after an early validation check. This pattern is fragile: if a new DType variant is added, the early check might be updated but the match arm might not, causing a panic.

## Problem
The DType enum has 9 variants (F64, F32, F16, BF16, I64, I32, Bool, Complex64, Complex128). Functions that only handle a subset (e.g., floating-point types) use an early if-guard then unreachable!() in the catch-all. A better pattern is to use a helper that converts the subset to a sub-enum, making the match exhaustive at compile time.

## Plan
1. Create typed helper enums or use existing type-level dispatch patterns:
   - FloatingDType { F64, F32, F16, BF16 } with TryFrom<DType>
   - NumericDType { F64, F32, I64, I32 } with TryFrom<DType>
2. Convert the most dangerous unreachable!() sites (those in hot paths like dispatch and backward) to use these sub-enums
3. The match becomes exhaustive and the compiler catches missing arms on enum changes
4. Less critical sites (test helpers, edge cases) can keep unreachable!() with messages

## Acceptance Criteria
- At least the 14 unreachable!() sites in ft-dispatch are converted to exhaustive matches
- New DType variants cause compile errors (not runtime panics) in dispatch code
- All existing tests pass

## Dependencies
- Depends on: frankentorch-dwy (DenseTensor::storage panic fix) -- both touch DType handling
- Should be done AFTER the storage() refactor to avoid merge conflicts

## Reasoning
This is the highest-leverage defense-in-depth change. It converts 14 potential runtime panics into compile-time errors, which aligns with the DAC correctness doctrine and Rust's zero-cost abstraction philosophy.
close_reason: Replaced all 14 ft-dispatch DType/promotion unreachable!() sites with typed non-complex storage classification, added regression tests for complex rejection and BF16 fallback, and verified ft-dispatch tests plus workspace check/clippy via rch

title: tensor_cumulative_trapezoid severs autograd; same composition as trapezoid + cumsum
description:
tensor_cumulative_trapezoid at lib.rs:15689 is the cumulative variant of trapezoid: instead of summing all weighted-avg pairs it returns the running cumulative sum. The body extracts values, computes the cumulative sum in plain f64, and rebuilds a non-grad leaf. PyTorch's torch.cumulative_trapezoid IS differentiable.

Same composition as tensor_trapezoid (frankentorch-b4ys) but with tensor_cumsum at the end instead of tensor_sum_dim:
   y0 = narrow(y, dim, 0, n-1)
   y1 = narrow(y, dim, 1, n-1)
   avg = (y0 + y1) * 0.5
   dx = (x1 - x0) if x else 1
   result = cumsum(avg * dx, dim)
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_cumulative_trapezoid through narrow + add + mul + cumsum primitives — same recipe as tensor_trapezoid (b4ys) but with cumsum instead of sum_dim. All ops autograd-aware. Added cumulative_trapezoid_propagates_gradient_through_y regression test (y=[1,2,3,4] → cumulative=[1.5, 4.0, 7.5]; under sum loss, grad_y=[1.5, 2.5, 1.5, 0.5] computed via reverse-cumulative routing). ft-api lib tests: 1173.

title: tensor_aminmax severs autograd; composable through min_dim/max_dim
description:
tensor_aminmax at lib.rs:15031 computes both min and max along a dim. Output is non-grad leaves. PyTorch's torch.aminmax IS differentiable through both branches (gradient routes via argmin/argmax to the corresponding input indices).

tensor_min_dim and tensor_max_dim already exist and are autograd-aware (they delegate to the tape's max_dim / min_dim primitives). Compose tensor_aminmax through them — discarding the indices since aminmax doesn't return them.

Surfaced during /reality-check pass continuing from cummax/cummin (wkkw).
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_aminmax through tensor_min_dim + tensor_max_dim (autograd-aware via the tape's argmin/argmax-routing reductions). Discarded the index outputs since aminmax doesn't return them. Added aminmax_propagates_gradient_via_argmin_argmax_routing regression test. ft-api lib tests: 1162.

title: Add metamorphic property tests: sigmoid complement, exp/log roundtrip, abs of product
description:
Extend ft-conformance metamorphic suite with three more invariants:

1. fuzz_metamorphic_sigmoid_complement_sums_to_one — sigmoid(x) + sigmoid(-x) == 1 within ULP envelope. The two sides of the sigmoid curve sum to 1 by definition.

2. fuzz_metamorphic_exp_log_roundtrip — log(exp(x)) ≈ x for x in a safe range (-30..30) within a small ULP envelope.

3. fuzz_metamorphic_abs_product_equals_product_of_abs — abs(x * y) == abs(x) * abs(y) bit-exactly (sign-bit math).

title: tensor_trapezoid severs autograd; composable through narrow/add/mul/sum_dim
description:
tensor_trapezoid at lib.rs:15607 computes the trapezoidal-rule integral along a dim. PyTorch's torch.trapezoid IS differentiable — it's linear in y (and bilinear-ish in x via dx pre-computation).

Composable through autograd primitives:
   y0 = narrow(y, dim, 0, n-1)
   y1 = narrow(y, dim, 1, n-1)
   avg = (y0 + y1) * 0.5
   dx = if x is provided: x1 - x0 (similar narrow). else: 1.0 broadcast.
   weighted = avg * dx
   integral = sum_dim(weighted, dim)

All ops autograd-aware so gradients flow to y (and x when provided). Surfaced during /reality-check pass.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_trapezoid through narrow + add + mul + sum_dim primitives. Math: y0 = narrow(y, dim, 0, n-1); y1 = narrow(y, dim, 1, n-1); avg = (y0 + y1) * 0.5; dx = (x1 - x0) if x else 1; integral = sum_dim(avg * dx, dim). All ops autograd-aware. Added trapezoid_propagates_gradient_through_y regression test (y=[1,2,3,4], uniform spacing → integral=7.5, grad_y=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5]). ft-api lib tests: 1172.

title: torch.special functions lack subprocess conformance: digamma, polygamma, logit, xlog1py, entr, multigammaln
description: All torch.special.* functions in ft-api/lib.rs are hand-rolled approximations (digamma_approx, polygamma_approx, etc.) but lack subprocess conformance against scipy.special. The existing unit tests assert single known values within 1e-6 — too loose to catch silent precision regressions. Add subprocess conformance harnesses comparing each against scipy.special.{digamma, polygamma, logit, xlog1py, entr, multigammaln}. Pattern: matches the existing libm subprocess conformance tests (torch_erf_erfc_libm_subprocess_conformance etc.) — ctypes-from-libm or scipy-via-numpy oracle, ULP-tolerant comparison.
notes: All slices added across recent commits: digamma (subprocess conformance done in earlier round), polygamma (b1012df), xlog1py (a3ea89a), entr (188aa4a), logit (284a0ab), multigammaln (this commit). Each test fail-loud against scipy.special with ULP / relative-tolerance comparators. Total ~150 inputs covered across the six harnesses.

title: functional_embedding severs autograd via tensor_values + non-grad rebuild
description:
ft-api functional_embedding (line 5411) extracts tensor_values from both indices and weight, looks up rows in plain f64, and rebuilds via session.tensor_variable(result, out_shape, false). This silently severs autograd through the weight tensor — breaks training for any model that uses F.embedding directly (transformers using token embeddings outside the nn.Embedding wrapper, custom layers, etc.).

The corresponding nn.Embedding module impl (Module for Embedding in ft-nn) already composes via tensor_reshape + tensor_index_select; functional_embedding should do the same.

Fix: compose via tensor_reshape(input, [n_indices]) + tensor_index_select(weight, 0, flat_indices) + tensor_reshape to [*input_shape, embedding_dim].

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs functional_embedding (line 5411).

title: affine_grid lacks autograd: linear in theta, easy backward [child of 3v6e]
description:
tensor_affine_grid currently has a fail-loud guard rejecting requires_grad=true theta. Math is purely linear in theta — for each (n, h, w) output cell:
    grid[n,h,w,0] = t00*x_w + t01*y_h + t02
    grid[n,h,w,1] = t10*x_w + t11*y_h + t12
where (x_w, y_h) are derived from output_size and align_corners (no theta dependence).

Backward (vector-Jacobian):
    grad_theta[n,0,0] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,0] * x_w
    grad_theta[n,0,1] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,0] * y_h
    grad_theta[n,0,2] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,0]
    grad_theta[n,1,0] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,1] * x_w
    grad_theta[n,1,1] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,1] * y_h
    grad_theta[n,1,2] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,1]

Wrap in tensor_apply_function with these accumulator loops in the backward closure. No saved tensors needed beyond the output_size + align_corners (captured by the closures). Standard PyTorch parity — torch.nn.functional.affine_grid supports backward.

Child of frankentorch-3v6e.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Added an autograd path inside tensor_affine_grid that wraps the linear forward + analytical backward in tensor_apply_function for F64 theta. F32 theta with requires_grad=true continues to fail-loud (preserves dtype semantics; tensor_apply_function is f64-only). Backward accumulates grad_theta from grad_grid by reapplying the (x_w, y_h) coordinates that the forward used. Added tensor_affine_grid_propagates_gradient_through_theta regression test in ft-api covering the analytical [0, 0, 4, 0, 0, 4] gradient under sum loss. Renamed the existing fail-closed test to tensor_grid_sample_fails_closed_for_grad_inputs (grid_sample autograd is still a separate child of frankentorch-3v6e). ft-api lib tests: 1137.

title: ft-nn LPPool1d/LPPool2d sever autograd; composable via unfold + abs + pow + sum_dim
description:
LPPool1d at lib.rs:12086 and LPPool2d compute (sum |x|^p)^(1/p) over each kernel window. Body extracts values, computes manually in plain f64, rebuilds non-grad leaf. PyTorch's torch.nn.LPPool* IS differentiable.

Composable through tensor_unfold (autograd-aware after frankentorch-hc18) + abs + pow + sum_dim + pow:
   for LPPool1d: unfold input on the length dim → [N, C, out_len, kernel_size]
   abs(unfolded), pow(norm_type), sum_dim(over kernel_size dim), pow(1/norm_type)
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed both LPPool1d and LPPool2d through tensor_unfold + abs + pow + sum_dim + pow (all autograd-aware after the unfold fix in frankentorch-hc18). For LPPool2d, nested unfold over H then W yields a [N, C, oh, ow, kh, kw] tensor that's reduced via two sum_dim calls. ft-nn lib tests: 517.

title: Add E2E training test for ConvTranspose2d upsampler
description:
Add a perfect-e2e regression for ConvTranspose2d (the recently-fixed zjf6 functional path). Trains a learnable transposed conv to map a 1x1x2x2 input up to 1x1x4x4, validating the scatter+pad+matmul backward path end-to-end.

Setup:

- ConvTranspose2d(1, 1, 2x2, stride 2)
- Output: 1x1x4x4
- MSE against target
- Adam lr=0.05 for 200 steps
- Assert final loss < initial * 0.1

title: Linear algebra ops lack subprocess conformance: svd, qr, cholesky, det, slogdet, inv, pinverse, solve, eigh, lstsq, matrix_norm
description: FEATURE_PARITY.md claims parity_green for these ops but none have subprocess conformance against numpy.linalg / scipy.linalg. Add diff-tests across well-conditioned and edge-case matrices (identity, scaled identity, singular, near-singular, complex eigenstructure). Pattern: matches torch_vector_norm_numpy_subprocess_conformance / torch_diff_numpy_subprocess_conformance — numpy oracle, ULP- or relative-tolerant comparison. Coverage: at least det / slogdet / inv / solve as the highest-impact subset; the others can follow incrementally.
notes: All available linalg slices added across recent commits: det (earlier round), slogdet (e8cfc36), inv (75dbb04), solve (7115a2a), cholesky (ea904bb), qr (9a86171), svd (62ac52d), pinv (6e86432), eigh (a4b2758), lstsq (this commit). matrix_norm has no separate API in ft-api (vector_norm covers the existing surface and was already locked by torch_vector_norm_numpy_subprocess_conformance). Ten subprocess harnesses against numpy.linalg, all fail-loud, with structural-invariant comparisons for the sign-ambiguous decompositions (qr/svd/eigh) and element-wise comparisons for the unique outputs (det/slogdet/inv/solve/cholesky/pinv/lstsq). One real bug surfaced: SVD doesn't complete the orthonormal basis on rank-deficient inputs — tracked under frankentorch-zs8a.

title: Add CUDA/GPU execution backend
description:
FrankenTorch currently exposes `Device::Cuda` tags and device-guard validation, but all real execution remains CPU-only. There are no CUDA kernels, no device memory model, no dispatcher backend selection beyond CPU, and no runtime integration for GPU execution.

Scope for eventual closure:
1. Add concrete GPU storage/device abstractions beyond enum tagging
2. Implement CUDA dispatch keys and backend routing
3. Port core kernels (elementwise, reductions, matmul, indexing) to GPU
4. Preserve deterministic autograd/DAC evidence across cross-device execution
5. Add conformance and benchmark coverage for CPU↔GPU parity

This bead exists so FEATURE_PARITY.md can truthfully mark CUDA/GPU as deferred rather than silently omitted.

title: masked_scatter all-false fast path drops source zero-gradient
description: Reviewer finding in crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs. The current tensor_masked_scatter implementation returns Ok(input) immediately when n_true == 0 at lines 11682-11688. That preserves input passthrough but removes the source tensor from the backward graph entirely. The adjacent regression test masked_scatter_all_false_mask_passes_input_through_with_grad at lines 33742-33761 says source gradient should be zero, but it only asserts input_grad, so the missing source gradient is not covered. Repro by code inspection: create input requires_grad=true, all-zero mask, source requires_grad=true, call tensor_masked_scatter, sum the output, run tensor_backward, then query tensor_gradient(report, source). Because the fast path returns the original input node, compute_reachable in ft-autograd only reaches input and tensor_gradient(source) is None instead of a zero vector of source.numel(). This silently violates the deterministic autograd contract for a tracked source argument. Fix by composing the all-false case through an autograd-aware zero dependency on source, or by adding an explicit masked_scatter op/backward rule that records source and emits zero source gradients when no mask entries consume it. Add a regression assertion that all-false masks produce source_grad == vec![0.0; source_numel].
close_reason: Removed the n_true==0 fast-path return-input shortcut for non-empty source — the gather + where composition now runs unconditionally for source_numel >= 1, so source stays in the backward graph and tensor_gradient(source) returns zeros(source.shape) instead of None. Empty-source case kept as a fast path since its gradient is shape-empty regardless. Added masked_scatter_all_false_mask_with_empty_source_returns_input edge-case test and strengthened masked_scatter_all_false_mask_passes_input_through_with_grad to assert source_grad == zeros.

title: functional_normalize severs autograd; composable through abs/pow/sum_dim/div
description:
functional_normalize at lib.rs:5151 computes y = x / max(||x||_p along dim, eps) but the body extracts values, divides in plain f64, and rebuilds a requires_grad=false leaf — silently severing autograd through every L2-normalization layer. PyTorch's torch.nn.functional.normalize IS differentiable.

Compose through autograd primitives:
   abs_x   = tensor_abs(input)
   pow_x   = tensor_pow(abs_x, p)
   sum_x   = tensor_sum_dim(pow_x, dim)
   norm    = tensor_pow(sum_x, 1.0 / p)
   norm_c  = tensor_clamp_min(norm, eps)
   norm_kd = tensor_unsqueeze(norm_c, dim)
   norm_b  = tensor_expand(norm_kd, original_shape)
   y       = tensor_div(input, norm_b)

All ops are autograd-aware so gradients flow back to input. Sister of frankentorch-7yvv (dropout severance) — both are critical for training paths.
notes:
Fixed in this commit. Composed functional_normalize through abs/pow/sum_dim/clamp_min/reshape/expand/div primitives — same observable y = x / max(||x||_p along dim, eps) but with the autograd tape preserved.

The norm reduction is reshaped to a keepdim=true-style broadcast shape (input's shape with dim set to 1) so the subsequent expand + div work uniformly across 1-D and higher-rank inputs.

Added functional_normalize_propagates_gradient_through_input regression test for input=[3,4] L2-normalize on dim=0:
   y = [0.6, 0.8] under sum loss → grad = [(1 - y_i * sum_y) / ||x||_2] = [0.032, -0.024]
asserts to 1e-12.

ft-api lib tests: 1156.

title: Add end-to-end training example and integration test
description:
FrankenTorch has e2e training loop tests buried in ft-optim's test suite, but there is no standalone, documented example showing users how to train a model. This is critical for proving the system works end-to-end and for user onboarding.

Create a comprehensive integration test (in ft-conformance or a new examples/ directory) that:

1. Defines a 2-layer MLP: Linear(784, 128) -> ReLU -> Linear(128, 10)
2. Generates synthetic MNIST-like data using the existing TensorDataset/DataLoader
3. Trains for 5 epochs using Adam optimizer with learning rate scheduling (StepLR or CosineAnnealing)
4. Tracks loss per epoch and asserts it decreases monotonically (with tolerance for noise)
5. Uses no_grad context for evaluation pass
6. Saves model state_dict to file, loads it back, verifies predictions match
7. Uses BatchNorm or LayerNorm in the architecture to exercise normalization paths
8. Logs: epoch number, batch count, loss per epoch, final accuracy estimate, elapsed time

This test exercises the full stack: ft-data (DataLoader) -> ft-nn (modules) -> ft-api (forward/backward) -> ft-autograd (gradient computation) -> ft-optim (parameter update) -> ft-serialize (save/load).

If any component fails to compose correctly, this test catches it.

Tests:
- The example IS the test: it should be a #[test] fn in ft-conformance or similar
- Assert loss decreases over 5 epochs
- Assert save/load round-trip produces identical predictions
- Assert no_grad eval context doesn't corrupt gradient state
- Log detailed per-epoch metrics for debugging
close_reason: Added 5 E2E integration tests exercising the full stack: MLP regression with DataLoader/Adam/StepLR, binary classification with cross-entropy, save/load round-trip verification, no_grad eval safety, dataloader epoch shuffle. All pass.

title: MaxUnpool1d/2d/3d sever autograd; scatter-then-gather backward needed
description:
MaxUnpool1d (line 6149), MaxUnpool2d (line 6237), and MaxUnpool3d (line 6337) all extract input values, scatter them via indices in plain f64, and rebuild requires_grad=false leaves. PyTorch's torch.nn.MaxUnpool* IS differentiable.

Backward: each output position came from a single input position (via the indices) — gather grad_output from those positions back to grad_input. Sister of repeat_interleave / diag_embed / unfold (gather-scatter pattern, except here forward is scatter and backward is gather, so the mapping inverts).

Each variant has the same forward/backward structure with different spatial indexing.
notes: Fixed in this commit. All three MaxUnpool variants (1d/2d/3d) now wrap their forward in tensor_apply_function with the analytical scatter-then-gather backward. The (input_pos → output_pos) indices mapping is captured by both forward and backward closures; backward gathers grad_output from the destination positions back to grad_input. ft-nn lib tests: 517.

title: ft-nn loss modules sever autograd: HingeEmbeddingLoss, FocalLoss, MultiMarginLoss, others
description:
Multiple loss modules in ft-nn extract values, compute the per-element loss in plain f64, and rebuild a non-grad leaf via tensor_variable(_, _, false). The subsequent reduction (mean/sum/none) operates on this severed leaf so gradients never reach the input.

Affected (line numbers from current main):
  - HingeEmbeddingLoss::forward_hinge (lib.rs:11003)
  - FocalLoss / its forward (lib.rs:~11800, near 11810)
  - MultiMarginLoss (lib.rs:~11827)
  - Other reduction-style loss modules in the 11800-12100 range

PyTorch's torch.nn.HingeEmbeddingLoss / FocalLoss / MultiMarginLoss are all differentiable. Each loss has a closed-form composition through autograd-aware primitives.

Children: each loss gets its own fix bead. Sister of frankentorch-2med (the session-method loss umbrella that closed earlier).
notes: Children landed: HingeEmbeddingLoss (8530), MultiMarginLoss (1s4q), SoftMarginLoss (fjao), FocalLoss multi-class softmax variant (kxcz), MultiLabelMarginLoss (r38w). Each loss module now composes through autograd-aware primitives instead of severing via tensor_values + tensor_variable(false).

title: tensor_diag_embed is severed-autograd: linear backward, easy fix
description:
tensor_diag_embed at lib.rs:3444 places a 1-D input vector onto the diagonal (or sub/super-diagonal) of a zero-initialized 2-D matrix. PyTorch's torch.diag_embed is differentiable. The forward is purely linear in the input:
   output[row, col] = input[i] if (row, col) is on the requested diagonal else 0
so the backward is just the diagonal-extraction:
   grad_input[i] = grad_output[row, col]
where (row, col) is the same position the forward placed input[i] in.

Wrap in tensor_apply_function with this analytical backward. Save offset and k for backward; or capture via closure. Surfaced during /reality-check pass scanning for the values-then-non-grad-leaf pattern.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped tensor_diag_embed in tensor_apply_function with diagonal-extraction backward: grad_input[i] = grad_output[row, col] for the same (row, col) the forward placed input[i]. Captured offset (positive vs negative) and abs_offset via closure (Copy-by-move). Added diag_embed_propagates_gradient_via_diagonal_extraction regression test using a weighted sum over a 3x3 main-diagonal embedding (input [1,2,3] gets gradient [1,5,9] = weights[i,i]). ft-api lib tests: 1145.

title: tensor_combinations severs autograd; gather-then-scatter backward
description:
tensor_combinations at lib.rs:4169 enumerates r-element combinations from a 1-D input (with or without replacement). Each output cell is a copy of one input element via a combinatorial mapping. PyTorch's torch.combinations IS differentiable.

Same pattern as cartesian_prod / unfold: pre-compute the (output_pos → input_pos) mapping during the recursive combo enumeration, then wrap in tensor_apply_function with gather-scatter backward.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped tensor_combinations in tensor_apply_function with gather-then-scatter backward. Pre-computed (output_pos → input_pos) mapping during the recursive combo enumeration (using indices instead of values), captured in both forward and backward closures. title: tensor_linalg_pinv: autograd via normal-equation formulation for full-rank A
description:
tensor_linalg_pinv in ft-api (line 12640) calls tensor_linalg_svd internally which now fails loud on requires_grad inputs (frankentorch-rl4g). This breaks pinv in autograd flows entirely.

Implement an autograd-aware pinv path for full-rank A:
- m >= n (full column rank): A^+ = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T
- m <= n (full row rank): A^+ = A^T (A A^T)^{-1}

Both compose through tensor_transpose + tensor_matmul + tensor_linalg_inv (all autograd-aware after recent fixes). The SVD-based path stays for non-grad inputs (handles rank-deficient cases properly).

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_linalg_pinv (line 12640).

title: tensor_scatter_reduce severs autograd via tensor_values + non-grad rebuild
description:
tensor_scatter_reduce in ft-api (line 16249) extracts both input and src tensor_values, runs the reduction in plain f64, and rebuilds via tensor_variable(_, _, false). Silently severs autograd. PyTorch supports gradients for reduce='sum'/'mean'/'max'/'min'.

Proper fix: compose the 'sum' and 'mean' modes via tensor_scatter_add (autograd-aware) — sum with the source, then divide by (1 + count) for mean. 'max'/'min' modes need argmax-routing via apply_function.

Interim fix: add a fail-loud guard on requires_grad input/src so the silent severance becomes loud.

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_scatter_reduce (line 16249).

title: tensor_linalg_solve severs autograd via direct LU kernel + non-grad rebuild
description:
tensor_linalg_solve in ft-api (line 11899) directly calls the LU factorize + LU solve kernels and rebuilds the result via session.tensor_variable(_, _, false). This silently severs autograd through every linear solve — used in normalizing flows, Bayesian models, optimization layers, GP regression, etc.

Fix: compose via tensor_linalg_inv + tensor_matmul. The linalg_inv was made autograd-aware in frankentorch-frq7 (-Y^T grad Y^T backward); composing X = inv(A) @ B delivers the standard gradient:
  dL/dB = A^{-T} dL/dX
  dL/dA = -A^{-T} dL/dX X^T = -A^{-T} (dL/dX) X^T

Trade-off: forward pass uses explicit inv instead of LU solve, slightly less numerically stable for ill-conditioned A. But the inv-based formulation matches PyTorch's autograd backward path, so gradient correctness is the same.

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_linalg_solve (line 11899).

title: tensor_linalg_cond severs autograd via tensor_values + non-grad rebuild
description:
tensor_linalg_cond in ft-api (line 12858) severs autograd in both code paths:

1. p=±2 path: extracts svdvals to f64, picks sigma_max/sigma_min by index, computes ratio in plain f64, rebuilds via tensor_variable(_, _, false). Severs gradients to the input matrix through the SVD.

2. p != ±2 path: invokes tensor_matrix_norm (autograd-aware) twice but then extracts both norms to f64 scalars and multiplies in plain f64, rebuilding a non-grad leaf. The two intermediate norm tensors carry gradients but the multiplication is done outside autograd.

Fix:
- p=±2: use tensor_narrow + tensor_reshape to extract sigma_max (first element) and sigma_min (last element) as 1-element tensors, then tensor_div them. Handle the zero-divisor case via a small epsilon clamp or fail-loud guard since gradient at sigma=0 is undefined anyway.
- p != ±2: drop the two tensor_values calls; just call tensor_mul(norm_a, norm_inv) directly.

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_linalg_cond (line 12858).

title: tensor_xlog1py masks NaN from y when x==0 (scipy propagates)
description: scipy.special.xlog1py(0.0, NaN) returns NaN — it short-circuits to 0 only when x==0 AND !isnan(y). FrankenTorch's tensor_xlog1py composes through where(x==0, 0, x*log1p(y)), so the where branch swallows NaN from y whenever x==0. This diverges from scipy/PyTorch on the (x=0, y=NaN) case. Fix: extend the mask to also exclude NaN y, e.g. where(x==0 & !isnan(y), 0, x*log1p(y)). Surfaced by torch_xlog1py_scipy_subprocess_conformance during the frankentorch-b5of work; current conformance test trims the failing case so the rest of the harness can land. Severity: low (NaN edge case, no observed downstream impact in tests), but breaks bit-exactness against scipy at the y=NaN boundary.
notes: Fixed in this commit: tightened the where mask in tensor_xlog1py from (x == 0) to (x == 0 && !isnan(y)) by composing tensor_eq + tensor_isnan + tensor_sub + tensor_mul. Now scipy / torch / FrankenTorch all return NaN at (0, NaN) instead of FrankenTorch's prior 0. Re-enabled the (0.0, NaN) case in torch_xlog1py_scipy_subprocess_conformance and added xlog1py_propagates_nan_from_y_when_x_is_zero regression test in ft-api.

title: Convert DenseTensor::storage() panic to Result return type
description:
## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) found a single production panic!() in ft-core/src/lib.rs:971-974:

```rust
panic!(
    "called storage() on {:?} DenseTensor; use typed_storage() or contiguous_values_as_f64()",
    other.dtype()
)
```

This fires when `.storage()` is called on a non-F64 DenseTensor. Since FrankenTorch supports F32, F16, BF16, Bool, I32, I64, Complex64, Complex128 dtypes, this panic is reachable in normal usage — it is NOT a "should never happen" guard.

## Problem
A library crate should never panic on valid inputs. This violates the Deterministic Autograd Contract (DAC) principle of fail-closed behavior: panics are not catchable in a structured way and produce undefined recovery state.

## Plan
1. Change `fn storage(&self) -> &[f64]` to `fn storage(&self) -> Result<&[f64], DenseTensorError>` (or rename to `storage_f64()` and return `Result`)
2. Add a new `DenseTensorError::UnsupportedStorageAccess { dtype: DType }` variant if not already present
3. Update ALL callers of `.storage()` in ft-autograd, ft-api, ft-kernel-cpu, ft-dispatch to handle the Result
4. Run full workspace tests + clippy to verify

## Acceptance Criteria
- Zero panic!() calls in non-test production code across all crates
- All callers propagate the error properly
- Existing tests still pass

## Risk
Medium refactor — `.storage()` may be called in many places. Use `ast-grep` to find all call sites before starting.
close_reason: DenseTensor::storage() now returns Result with UnsupportedStorageAccess errors; updated ft-api call sites and verified workspace test/check/clippy via rch

Add an autograd-aware path that composes lstsq via tensor_linalg_pinv + tensor_matmul. The SVD-based path stays for non-grad inputs to handle rank-deficient cases.

title: log_softmax kernel loses ~1000 ULPs at large magnitudes due to operation ordering
description:
log_softmax_dim_tensor_contiguous_f64 (and the f32 variant) computes
    log_sum_exp = max + log(sum(exp(x - max)))
    output[i] = x[i] - log_sum_exp
which evaluates as x[i] - (max + log(sum_exp)). When x ~ 1000, max ~ 1000, the intermediate max + log(sum_exp) is ~1000-ish but the final result is ~−2; the catastrophic cancellation between two ~1000-magnitude values discards ~13 digits of mantissa.

scipy.special.log_softmax is mathematically equivalent but algebraically rearranged:
    output[i] = (x[i] - max) - log(sum_exp)
This keeps the intermediate small (~ −2 for x near max) and preserves full f64 precision.

Surfaced by torch_softmax_scipy_subprocess_conformance during the /reality-check pass: at large_positive_1d=[1000,1001,1002], FrankenTorch returns -2.4076059644444285 vs scipy's -2.4076059644443806 — a diff of ~5e-14 (about 1000 ULPs), well above the libm noise floor.

Fix: rewrite the output loop to compute (x - max) - log(sum_exp). The fast path (inner_size == 1) and the general path both need the change. Affects log_softmax_dim_tensor_contiguous_f64 in crates/ft-kernel-cpu/src/lib.rs:2134 and the f32 variant at line 5729.

Severity: medium — the precision gap matters for cross-entropy training with logits in extreme magnitudes (e.g., overconfident classifiers in late training). Most practical inputs stay within ~1e2 magnitude where the gap is negligible.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Rewrote log_softmax_dim_tensor_contiguous_f64 (and the f32 variant) to compute (x - max) - log(sum_exp) instead of x - (max + log(sum_exp)). The two are algebraically identical but the new form keeps the intermediate small (~result-magnitude) instead of ~max-magnitude, which preserves full f64/f32 precision through the subtraction. softmax conformance now passes large_positive_1d and large_negative_1d cases bit-for-bit with scipy.

title: ft-nn PReLU and shrink activations sever autograd
description: Reviewer finding in crates/ft-nn/src/lib.rs after the recent activation-gradient repair wave. Several activation modules still read tensor values, compute in plain f64, and return session.tensor_variable(..., false), so a requires_grad=true input produces a fresh untracked output and tensor_backward cannot propagate gradients. Concrete sites: PReLU::forward lines 3576-3642 reads both input and its learnable weight via tensor_values and returns an untracked leaf, so both input gradients and the PReLU weight gradient are dropped; Softshrink lines 3918-3938, Hardshrink lines 3966-3978, Tanhshrink lines 3991-4000, Softsign lines 4014-4023, Softmin lines 4046-4057, and RReLU lines 4154-4168 have the same severed-input pattern. Existing tests for these modules are forward-only (for example prelu_default_forward/prelu_has_parameters/prelu_multichannel, softshrink_shrinks, tanhshrink_values, softsign_bounded, softmin_is_softmax_of_negated, rrelu_module_*), and the full ft-nn test suite still passes: rch exec -- cargo test -p ft-nn reported 508 passed on 2026-04-29. Repro by code inspection: create a requires_grad=true input, run PReLU::forward (or any listed activation), reduce with tensor_sum, then tensor_backward; tensor_gradient for input is absent because the forward output is a requires_grad=false leaf. For PReLU, tensor_gradient for self.weight is also absent even though parameters() exposes that learnable slope. Fix by composing each activation through existing autograd-aware primitives (e.g. PReLU via tensor_where/tensor_mul with broadcasted weight; shrink ops via tensor_where; Softmin via tensor_neg or 0-input subtraction then tensor_softmax; RReLU via leaky_relu-style composition) and add regression tests asserting input gradients, plus weight gradients for PReLU.
close_reason: Composed all 7 listed activation modules through autograd-aware primitives: PReLU via tensor_reshape+tensor_expand+tensor_mul+tensor_where (now propagates to both input and learnable weight); Softshrink/Hardshrink via nested tensor_where + tensor_abs/tensor_sub/tensor_add + tensor_gt/tensor_lt; Tanhshrink via tensor_sub(input, tanh(input)); Softsign via tensor_div(input, 1+abs); Softmin via tensor_neg + tensor_softmax; RReLU via where(x>=0, x, slope*x). Added 8 gradient regression tests asserting analytical d/dx for each module (plus PReLU weight grads, both single-slope and multichannel). All 11 existing forward tests continue to pass — forward parity preserved.

title: softmax / log_softmax lack subprocess conformance against torch/numpy
description:
tensor_softmax and tensor_log_softmax are the canonical activation/output functions for classification heads, attention layers, and most loss formulations. They have unit tests in ft-api but no subprocess conformance against an authoritative reference (torch.nn.functional.softmax / scipy.special.softmax / numpy). Bit-exact agreement matters because:

- Numerical stability: log_softmax(x) = log(softmax(x)) is computed via the max-subtraction trick to avoid exp overflow; the equivalence with the naive form must hold within libm precision across all inputs.
- Gradient stability: softmax/log_softmax forward/backward are coupled (dL/dx = softmax(x) - target for cross-entropy). Drift between FrankenTorch and PyTorch here corrupts every classification training run.
- Edge cases: large positive inputs (exp overflow risk), large negative (underflow), uniform input (all equal probabilities), single-element softmax (degenerate).

Add torch_softmax_torch_subprocess_conformance covering interior, large-magnitude, near-uniform, and edge-case inputs along multiple dims; assert ULP-tolerant equality against scipy.special.softmax (or numpy formulation if scipy unavailable). Same for log_softmax.

Bias toward filing-from-/reality-check: this is a pre-existing instance test gap, not a new bug.
notes: Closed by adding torch_softmax_scipy_subprocess_conformance covering interior, uniform, scalar, large-positive / large-negative (with max-subtract numerical stability), mixed-sign, and 1-D / 2-D / 3-D shapes across multiple dims. 8-ULP tolerance with 1e-15 absolute floor. Also surfaced the log_softmax precision bug tracked under frankentorch-ebrb (now fixed in the same commit).

title: Implement tensor_linalg_inv autograd: grad_A = -A_inv^T grad_out A_inv^T [child of tw0y]
description:
Wrap tensor_linalg_inv in tensor_apply_function with the standard matrix-calculus backward:
    grad_A = -Y^T @ grad_Y @ Y^T   where Y = A^{-1}
Save Y (the forward output) for backward to avoid recomputing the inverse. Output shape is [n, n] for n×n input. F64-only autograd path; F32 input with requires_grad continues to fail-loud (consistent with the affine_grid pattern).

Add tensor_linalg_inv_propagates_gradient regression test in ft-api: for diagonal A, the inverse is reciprocal-diag and the gradient under sum loss simplifies to a clean closed-form for verification.

title: tensor_renorm severs autograd; composable via permute/reshape/norm/clamp/div/mul
description:
tensor_renorm at lib.rs:15185 normalizes sub-tensors along a dim so their p-norm is bounded by maxnorm. The body extracts values, computes the per-slice norm in plain f64, conditionally scales, and rebuilds a non-grad leaf — silently severing autograd. PyTorch's torch.renorm IS differentiable (sub-gradient at the boundary norm == maxnorm).

Closed form for the scale factor:
   scale_d = maxnorm / max(norm_d, maxnorm)
which equals 1 when norm_d <= maxnorm and maxnorm/norm_d when norm_d > maxnorm.

Composable via:
   permute dim to position 0
   reshape to [dim_size, other_size]
   abs + pow + sum_dim(1) + pow(1/p)  → norm per slice
   denom = clamp_min(norm, maxnorm)
   scale = maxnorm / denom
   broadcast scale via unsqueeze + expand
   mul, reshape back, permute back

Used heavily in embedding regularization (torch.nn.Embedding(max_norm=...)). Surfaced during /reality-check pass.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_renorm through permute/reshape/abs/pow/sum_dim/clamp_min/div/mul/expand. The closed-form scale per slice is maxnorm / max(norm, maxnorm), which equals 1 when norm <= maxnorm and maxnorm/norm when norm > maxnorm — clamp_min(norm, maxnorm) gives exactly the denom we want. Strategy: permute dim to position 0, reshape to [dim_size, other_size], compute per-row L_p norm, scale each row, reshape + permute back. dim==0 case skips the (no-op) permutes. Added renorm_propagates_gradient_through_input regression test (mixed clamp / no-clamp rows; row 1 in identity branch gives grad=1, row 0 in scaled branch gives finite non-zero). ft-api lib tests: 1166.

title: tensor_unfold severs autograd; gather-then-scatter backward needed
description:
tensor_unfold at lib.rs:15083 extracts sliding windows. Each output element is a copy of an input element via a deterministic mapping (flat_out → in_flat). PyTorch's torch.Tensor.unfold IS differentiable.

Backward: scatter — grad_input[in_flat] += grad_output[flat_out] for each (flat_out, in_flat) pair the forward used. The mapping is deterministic given (dimension, size, step) and the input shape, so we can recompute it inside the backward closure.

Wrap in tensor_apply_function with this gather-then-scatter pattern.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped tensor_unfold in tensor_apply_function with gather-then-scatter backward. The (flat_out -> in_flat) mapping is computed once in the outer body and captured by both forward (gather) and backward (scatter) closures so the same routing is used in both directions; overlapping windows (step < size) accumulate via += in the scatter. Added unfold_propagates_gradient_via_scatter regression test (x=[1,2,3,4], size=3, step=1 → 2 overlapping windows, grad_x = [1,2,2,1] under sum loss). ft-api lib tests: 1164.

title: Quantized storage dtypes (QInt8/QUInt8) and QuantizedLinear
description:
Follow-up to frankentorch-4ak. The MVP fake-quantization slice landed in 4f6fff6 covers post-training calibration + QAT-style fake quantize using composed round/clamp ops, which keeps storage in the input dtype.

Remaining V1 work to fully match torch.quantization:
1. Add QInt8 / QUInt8 storage dtypes in ft-core (TensorStorage variant + DType enum + serde paths).
2. Add QuantizedTensorMeta carrying scale + zero_point as part of TensorMeta.
3. Add a QuantizedLinear module in ft-nn that stores INT8 weights and dequantizes on the fly (or invokes a fused INT8 matmul kernel when added).
4. Per-channel quantization (per-axis scale/zero_point vectors).
5. Conformance fixtures comparing against torch.quantization output on a couple of canonical layers.

Out of scope here: GPU INT8 kernels (tracked under frankentorch-c3d).

title: tensor_matrix_norm has severed-autograd + lacks numpy subprocess conformance
description:
tensor_matrix_norm exists in ft-api/src/lib.rs:11602 supporting fro / 1 / -1 / inf / -inf orderings. It extracts tensor values and rebuilds a requires_grad=false leaf, silently severing the autograd tape (same severed-autograd pattern as the cov / corrcoef / mode / quantile family that was fixed under frankentorch-1tax). PyTorch's torch.linalg.matrix_norm IS differentiable — for 'fro' it's just sqrt of sum of squares (composable through autograd primitives), and for the operator norms it's a max/min over column/row L1 sums (also composable through tensor_abs + tensor_sum_dim + tensor_max/min).

Also missed during the c36b closure: my earlier closure note claimed matrix_norm 'has no separate API in ft-api' — that was wrong, it does. It just wasn't covered by a subprocess conformance harness.

Two fixes needed:
  1. Compose tensor_matrix_norm through autograd primitives (or wrap in tensor_apply_function) so gradients flow.
  2. Add torch_matrix_norm_numpy_subprocess_conformance harness covering all five orderings.

Surfaced during a /reality-check pass scanning for severed-autograd patterns.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_matrix_norm through autograd primitives: 'fro' = sqrt(sum(A*A)) via tensor_mul + tensor_sum + tensor_sqrt; '1'/'-1'/'inf'/'-inf' = max/min over (sum_dim of tensor_abs) via tensor_abs + tensor_sum_dim + tensor_max_dim/min_dim. Output shape preserved as [1] via tensor_unsqueeze when reductions yield 0-D scalars. Added matrix_norm_frobenius_propagates_gradient and matrix_norm_inf_propagates_gradient_to_max_row regression tests in ft-api (1139 passing). Added torch_matrix_norm_numpy_subprocess_conformance covering all five orderings (fro / 1 / -1 / inf / -inf) across 12 matrices. Subprocess conformance count now 32 (was 31).

description:
Add a perfect-e2e regression for a typical CNN classifier head: Conv2d → MaxPool2d → Flatten → Linear. Validates that the autograd composition through narrow + cat + matmul (Conv2d), narrow + max_dim + cat (MaxPool2d), reshape (Flatten), and matmul (Linear) all compose correctly under Adam.
